VO_EN: The man sitting across from you will not survive the week.
You're in a cafe in Dubai.
The air smells like cardamom and traffic exhaust.
Outside the window, cranes turn slowly against the haze.
You're reading a newspaper you finished an hour ago.
But someone there trusts you with their life.
Slides his phone face-down across the table.
In cities you're not supposed to remember.
But you choose who goes back.
They train you at a place called the Farm.
You learn to walk into a room so that no one remembers you left.
You learn to build a face that fits any conversation.
The instructors never raise their voices.
They speak the way you'll learn to speak.
Like someone explaining the weather while a building burns.
Your first station is in a Gulf city.
The office looks like a consulting firm.
There's a reception desk with fake brochures.
Behind it, a steel door with no handle.
You find the crack in someone's loyalty.
Your first developmental contact is a telecom engineer.
He checks his watch every two minutes.
Wears the same grey jacket every time you meet.
You spend six months before the first real ask.
Of tea you don't really want.
Of building something that feels like trust.
The meeting ritual takes six hours.
Forty minutes in a cafe reading a newspaper.
The same man passes you twice.
You're trained to notice these things.
You're also trained to doubt what you notice.
Then you sit across from him.
That's how you know something is wrong.
Because in this work, nothing is never nothing.
You just convince them to go back.
The year the war starts, everything changes.
Vetting periods are cut from six months to three weeks.
You're told speed matters more than certainty.
You are told this is temporary.
It asks about a meeting you didn't report.
Mid-level officer with access to communications infrastructure.
The vetting period is three weeks.
He tells you about his daughter.
A fabricated detail designed to build rapport.
In your cable, he isn't a person.
A counterintelligence officer sits in on your briefing.
He takes notes you can't see.
He asks about a meeting from six months ago.
Someone at headquarters knows a detail they shouldn't.
You check who had access to your files.
The list is longer than you expected.
One morning, the channel lights up.
Your intelligence is passed to military planners.
Three days later, a damage assessment lands on your desk.
You zoom in on the image.
You promised to get him out.
The answer comes back in four words.
She's the same age as yours.
And exposure means more people like him.
But the information is too clean.
Too perfectly aligned with what headquarters wants to hear.
Because your career depends on production.
Because the alternative is admitting you don't know.
The network collapses three months later.
You read them on a screen in a windowless room.
One of them always ordered tea.
You fly home to suburban Virginia.
A split-level house near a good school.
Your daughter's drawings are on the fridge.
The encrypted phone stays in your jacket pocket.
On the coat rack by the door.
You check it under the dinner table.
When your wife is clearing plates.
When your daughter is talking about school.
No one looks at your hands.
One evening, your daughter looks up at you.
She asks: do you help people?
It's the most honest lie you've ever told.
You're not sure the answer is still true.
You're not sure it was ever true.
A ceasefire is announced on television.
Someone in the next room claps.
You're thinking about a grey jacket.
About a man who checked his watch every two minutes.
About tea that got cold because he was too nervous to drink it.
The chair across from you is empty.
There is no tea on the table.
The absence is louder than anything in the room.
This is how a war ends.
